1.name: SQL injection
1.fix: Use parameterized queries or ORM. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.

2.name: XSS - unescaped output
2.fix: Escape all user-supplied content before rendering in HTML.

3.name: Command injection
3.fix: Never pass user input to shell commands. Use subprocess with list args, not shell=True.

4.name: Path traversal
4.fix: Validate and sanitize all file paths from user input. Use path.resolve() and verify it stays within allowed directory.

5.name: Hardcoded credentials
5.fix: Move to environment variables. Rotate the exposed credential immediately.

6.name: Insecure deserialization
6.fix: Replace pickle/yaml.load/eval with safe alternatives. Use json.loads() or yaml.safe_load().

7.name: Insecure random for security context
7.fix: Use crypto.randomBytes() or secrets.token_hex() for security-sensitive randomness.

8.name: Missing CSRF protection
8.fix: Enable CSRF middleware. Verify CSRF token on all state-changing endpoints.

9.name: Sensitive data in logs
9.fix: Remove passwords, tokens, PII from log statements. Log correlation IDs instead.

10.name: Auth logic flaw (IDOR, broken access control)
10.ai_prompt: Check whether the function verifies that the requesting user has permission to access the specific resource being requested. Look for cases where a user could access another user's data by changing an ID parameter.
10.fix: Verify resource ownership or permission explicitly. Never rely on client-supplied IDs alone.

11.name: Insufficient input validation
11.ai_prompt: Check whether all externally-supplied values (user input, API responses, file contents) are validated for type, range, format, and length before use.
11.fix: Add explicit validation with clear error messages for all external input before it is used.

12.name: Error messages leaking internals
12.ai_prompt: Check whether error messages returned to callers include stack traces, database error details, internal file paths, or implementation details.
12.fix: Return generic error messages to callers. Log detailed errors server-side with correlation IDs.

18.name: AWS keys
18.fix: Revoke the key immediately in AWS IAM. Rotate all access. Audit CloudTrail for unauthorized use.

19.name: Private keys (RSA/SSH/PGP)
19.fix: Revoke and regenerate the key pair. Audit all systems where this key had access.

20.name: Database connection string with password
20.fix: Move to environment variable or secrets manager. Rotate the database password.

26.name: Critical CVE in direct dependency
26.fix: Update to patched version. If no patch exists, evaluate mitigating controls or alternative package.

27.name: High CVE in direct dependency
27.fix: Update to patched version in next sprint.

33.name: S3 bucket public access
33.fix: Set block_public_acls = true, block_public_policy = true, ignore_public_acls = true, restrict_public_buckets = true.

34.name: Security group 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive port
34.fix: Restrict ingress to specific IP ranges or security group IDs. Never allow 0.0.0.0/0 on ports 22, 3306, 5432, 6379, 27017.

40.name: Premature abstraction
40.ai_prompt: Look for: interfaces with only one implementation, factory classes that create only one type, strategy pattern with only one strategy, abstract base classes with one concrete subclass. These abstractions add complexity without current value.
40.fix: Remove the abstraction. Inline the single implementation. Re-introduce the abstraction when a second use case exists.

41.name: Unnecessary indirection
41.ai_prompt: Look for: wrapper functions that only call another function without adding logic, pass-through classes that delegate every method, adapter layers between things that don't need adapting.
41.fix: Remove the wrapper. Call the underlying implementation directly.

42.name: Speculative generality
42.ai_prompt: Look for: generic type parameters that are never instantiated with more than one type, configuration options that no caller currently sets, plugin systems with exactly one plugin, feature flags that have never been toggled.
42.fix: Remove the generality. Solve the specific problem. Generalize when the second case actually arrives.

43.name: God class or god function
43.ai_prompt: Look for: classes over 300 lines, functions over 40 lines, modules with more than 10 public exports. Evaluate whether the class/function has a single clear responsibility or is doing multiple unrelated things.
43.fix: Split along responsibility boundaries. Each class/function should do one thing you can name in a single sentence without 'and'.

44.name: Deep nesting
44.ai_prompt: Look for blocks nested deeper than 3 levels, nested ternary expressions, callback pyramids, deeply nested if/else chains.
44.fix: Use early returns (guard clauses), extract helper functions, or use async/await to flatten callback pyramids.

45.name: Excessive parameterization
45.ai_prompt: Look for functions with more than 4 parameters, boolean flags that switch behavior (flag arguments), options objects with more than 8 keys where most are rarely set.
45.fix: Group related parameters into a typed options object. Split functions with boolean flags into two specific functions.

50.name: Duplicated logic
50.ai_prompt: Look for the same logical pattern appearing 3 or more times across files. Semantic duplication counts even when variable names differ — look for structural similarity, not just textual copy-paste.
50.fix: Extract into a named utility function. The name should capture the concept, not the implementation.

51.name: Feature envy
51.ai_prompt: Look for methods that use more data and methods from another class than from their own class. This is a sign the method belongs in the other class.
51.fix: Move the method to the class whose data it primarily uses.

52.name: Shotgun surgery
52.ai_prompt: Examine the git diff: does this single logical change touch more than 5 unrelated files? Does a concept appear to be spread across many files when it should be centralized?
52.fix: Centralize the concept. One file or module should own each concept.

62.name: Circular dependencies
62.ai_prompt: Check whether any of the changed files create import cycles: A imports B which imports A, or longer chains. Look at import statements and identify any cycles introduced.
62.fix: Break the cycle by extracting the shared dependency into a third module that both can import, or by inverting the dependency direction.

63.name: Single responsibility violation
63.ai_prompt: For each changed class and module, ask: can you describe its responsibility in one sentence without using 'and'? If not, it violates single responsibility.
63.fix: Split the class or module along responsibility lines. Each piece should have one clear, nameable reason to change.

78.name: N+1 queries
78.ai_prompt: Look for loops that issue individual database queries on each iteration — fetching a list and then querying for related data per item. This is the N+1 problem.
78.fix: Use a JOIN, eager loading, or batch fetch to retrieve related data in a single query.

79.name: Unbounded queries
79.ai_prompt: Look for database queries without LIMIT or TOP clauses on endpoints that could return user-generated or growing data sets.
79.fix: Add LIMIT with a reasonable maximum. Add pagination for endpoints returning lists.

84.name: Undocumented public API
84.ai_prompt: Check whether every new public function, method, or exported symbol has a docstring or JSDoc comment describing what it does, its parameters, return value, and any exceptions it throws.
84.fix: Add docstring before the implementation. Write it first — if you can't describe the function, you don't know what you're building.
